Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Hello everyone!
I’m Pranav Kulkarni, and I’m excited to continue my journey with HumanAI as part of Google Summer of Code 2026. Last year, during GSoC 2025, I worked on building the foundation of the RenAIssance OCR pipeline which is a deep-learning based system designed for recognizing and preserving 17th-century Spanish historical documents.
The initial goal was to move beyond traditional OCR approaches and create a complete workflow capable of handling the challenges of historical texts. During GSoC 2025, I developed an end-to-end pipeline combining text-line detection using Mask R-CNN, TrOCR-based recognition, OCR correction experiments with Hunspell, T5, and LLMs, along with a human-in-the-loop annotation platform for expert correction and dataset improvement.
The pipeline followed the structure:
Scanned Document → Text Line Detection → OCR Recognition → Correction → Human Review → Final Transcription
This work resulted in a functional prototype that demonstrated how modern transformer-based models could be adapted for historical document digitization. However, while the system performed well, several real-world challenges remained: noisy scans, marginalia interference, unnecessary computational overhead, deployment limitations, and the need for a more optimized user-facing workflow.
Moving Forward: GSoC 2026 Goals
For GSoC 2026, the focus shifts from building the OCR pipeline to making it efficient and deployment-ready.
The objective is to transform the existing prototype into a complete optimized system by improving every stage of the pipeline. The major goals include:

• Developing a stronger preprocessing pipeline for degraded historical scans

• Adding layout-aware filtering and marginalia removal

• Optimizing the existing TrOCR model for lower inference time and memory usage

• Delivering a complete application where researchers can process documents through a clean interface
The final system aims to operate as:
Preprocessing → Layout Filtering (Marginalia Removal) → Text Line Extraction → OCR → Correction → User Application
Rather than simply improving recognition accuracy, the goal is to make the entire pipeline practical for historians, researchers, and digital archive projects.
Executive Summary: Midterm Progress
By the GSoC 2026 midterm milestone, the focus has been on evolving last year’s OCR prototype into a faster, cleaner, and more scalable end-to-end system.
The pipeline has been redesigned around structured document processing:
Raw Historical Page
→ Preprocessing
→ Marginalia Detection & Removal
→ Clean Text Region Extraction
→ Text Line Detection
→ TrOCR Recognition
Major progress includes building a robust preprocessing workflow, introducing layout-aware marginalia filtering, and optimizing the existing TrOCR model for improved inference speed and memory efficiency while preserving accuracy.
These improvements establish a stronger foundation for the second half of GSoC 2026, focused on correction systems, application integration, benchmarking, and delivering a complete deployable OCR platform for historical document transcription.
Marginalia Detection: Separating Notes from Main Text
Press enter or click to view image in full size

One of the major limitations observed in last year’s OCR pipeline was handling marginalia handwritten notes and annotations appearing around the main printed content. These regions can be important, but when processed together with the main text, they can disrupt text-line extraction, reading order reconstruction, and OCR quality.
To explore an automated solution, I framed marginalia detection as a single-class object detection problem. The models were trained using the Marginalia Training Data dataset, containing annotated early modern book pages from sources including the Archaeology of Reading (AoR), UCLA Clark Library, and National Library of Scotland collections.
Our aim here is detect handwritten marginal notes and generate bounding boxes around them before OCR processing.
Pipeline tested:
Raw Page
→ Image Preprocessing
→ Marginalia Detection Model
→ Bounding Box Filtering
→ Clean Text Region Extraction
I benchmarked 10 different models across two approaches:

• YOLO (Ultralytics): single-stage detectors optimized for fast inference

• LayoutParser / Detectron2: two-stage Faster R-CNN and Mask R-CNN models optimized for document layout understanding
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Observations: YOLOv8m achieved the best overall balance between accuracy and efficiency, while YOLO11m provided the fastest inference with the highest mAP@50. LayoutParser-based models, especially FasterRCNN-R101, produced more precise bounding boxes due to their region proposal architecture, which is useful when exact document boundaries matter.
I also experimented with post-processing strategies such as Soft-NMS to reduce duplicate overlapping detections, improving precision when marginal notes appeared close together.
While these results were promising, fully automated marginalia removal was still not reliable enough for integration into the final OCR pipeline. Historical documents vary heavily annotations differ in handwriting style, ink quality, placement, and density. In archival workflows, a false removal is worse than extra noise because it risks losing original historical information.
Therefore, instead of forcing an uncertain detection model into production, I moved toward a different approach: adding a manual main-text definition feature directly inside the preprocessing pipeline. This allows to select the primary text region when needed while maintaining predictable OCR behavior.
The complete preprocessing workflow and this user-guided filtering approach are discussed in the next section.
Building a Preprocessing Pipeline
Press enter or click to view image in full size

After experimenting with automated marginalia detection, the next focus was improving the input stage of the OCR pipeline itself. Historical documents are rarely clean and pages often contain uneven illumination, faded ink, scanning noise, page curvature, and alignment issues.
Instead of sending raw scanned pages directly into text-line detection and OCR, I built a dedicated preprocessing pipeline to generate cleaner and more consistent inputs.
The final preprocessing workflow:
Raw Scan
→ Skew Correction
→ Background Normalization
→ Denoising
→ Adaptive Binarization
→ Morphological Cleanup
→ OCR-ready Document
Core Preprocessing Steps
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Main stages of the OCR preprocessing workflow.Along with improving image quality, a major challenge was making preprocessing fast enough for an interactive application. The initial implementation produced good outputs but was computationally expensive, taking around 14.2 seconds per page, mainly due to full-resolution denoising and background normalization.
To solve this, I optimized multiple components of the pipeline:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Major algorithmic improvements reducing preprocessing latencyThese changes reduced preprocessing latency significantly:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

End-to-end preprocessing performance after optimizationThe optimized system can now preprocess a high-resolution 300 DPI document scan in under a second, making real-time interaction practical.
Manual Main Text Region Selection
The preprocessing pipeline also introduced an important feature motivated by the marginalia detection experiments: manual region selection.
Although object detection models achieved strong benchmark results, fully automated marginalia removal was not reliable enough for archival workflows. Removing the wrong region could permanently discard historically important content.
To solve this, I added an interactive Region of Interest (ROI) selector.
Users can:

• manually draw the main text regions

• define reading order through numbered selections

• apply the same layout template across multiple pages

• process only the selected regions through OCR
With these improvements, we can minimize the OCR workflow errors.
Optimizing TrOCR: Faster Inference Without Sacrificing Accuracy
The OCR backbone continues to use qantev/trocr-large-spanish, the transformer-based recognition model adapted for historical Spanish documents.
During GSoC 2025, the focus was maximizing OCR accuracy. However, for real-world deployment, processing speed and GPU efficiency became equally important. Instead of replacing the model with a smaller alternative and losing accuracy, I focused on optimizing the inference pipeline around the existing TrOCR-Large model.
The optimization process targeted multiple bottlenecks:

• Batched Inference: Processes multiple text-line crops together to improve GPU utilization.

• FP16 Mixed Precision: Reduces computation and memory requirements using half-precision operations.

• KV-Cache Decoding: Stores previous attention states during autoregressive decoding to avoid repeated computation.

• SDPA Attention Backend: Uses PyTorch’s optimized scaled dot-product attention kernels for faster transformer execution.

• FP8 Simulation Experiments: Evaluates lower precision execution strategies for future hardware acceleration.
Benchmarking was performed using qantev/trocr-large-spanish on historical Spanish text-line crops.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Incremental inference optimizations on qantev/trocr-large-spanish (T4
GPU, 4 pages, 92 text-line crops)Through these optimizations, the same TrOCR-Large model achieved around a 4× inference speedup with significantly lower memory usage while preserving transcription accuracy.
This allowed the OCR engine to move closer toward a feasible system capable of processing efficiently.
Integrating the Pipeline: Connecting Preprocessing with OCR
After optimizing the individual components, the next step was combining this year’s preprocessing improvements with the OCR system developed during GSoC 2025.
The previous pipeline already contained the core recognition workflow:
Text Line Detection
→ TrOCR Recognition
→ Correction Pipeline
→ Final Output
However, raw scanned pages were directly passed into the system, which made OCR performance highly dependent on input quality. In GSoC 2026, the pipeline was redesigned so that every document first passes through the preprocessing and layout filtering stages before recognition.
The updated workflow:
Raw Historical Document
→ Preprocessing Pipeline
→ Manual Region Selection / Text Line Detection
→ Optimized TrOCR Inference
→ OCR Output
The existing components were also modularized during integration. The TrOCR engine was separated into its own inference module with batching, FP16, KV-cache, and attention optimizations. The Detectron2-based text-line detector from last year was preserved and connected directly with the new preprocessing outputs.
The manual region selector was also integrated into this flow — user-defined regions are cropped from the cleaned document, processed in reading order, and passed through batched TrOCR inference automatically.
Conclusion
At the GSoC 2026 midterm stage, the RenAIssance OCR pipeline has progressed from last year’s working prototype into a more optimized and scalable document processing system.
The focus so far has been on building a faster preprocessing workflow, exploring layout-aware marginalia handling, optimizing TrOCR inference, and integrating these improvements with the existing OCR pipeline developed during GSoC 2025.
The current system now follows a complete flow.
With the core pipeline now in place, the second half of GSoC 2026 will focus on turning this system into a complete user-facing application. Upcoming work includes improving the interface, adding advanced correction layers, benchmarking the full pipeline, and preparing a deployable tool that researchers can use for historical document tr
